course: Tax Benefits of Residential Rental Property
note: This is an independent study aid, not the official course or tax advice; tax figures and rules change frequently, so always verify current IRS rules (e.g., IRS Publication 527) and consult a qualified tax professional before acting.

modules.title: Unit 1: Rental Income & Deductible Operating Expenses
modules.body:
Rental income is generally taxable in the year you receive it. It includes more than the monthly rent check: advance rent, payments to cancel a lease, tenant-paid expenses (such as a tenant paying your water bill in lieu of rent), and the fair-market value of property or services received instead of cash. Security deposits are NOT income when received if you intend to return them, but a deposit you keep (for example, to cover unpaid rent or damages) becomes income at that point. Rental income and expenses are typically reported on Schedule E.

Against that income, an owner may deduct the ordinary and necessary expenses of operating the rental. Common deductible operating expenses include mortgage interest, property taxes, insurance premiums, utilities you pay, property-management fees, HOA dues, advertising to find tenants, cleaning and maintenance, supplies, and legal or professional fees. These are deducted in the year incurred.

A key distinction is repairs versus improvements. A repair keeps the property in ordinary operating condition (fixing a leak, repainting, replacing a broken window) and is currently deductible. An improvement that betters the property, restores it, or adapts it to a new use (a new roof, an addition, a kitchen remodel) is a capital improvement: it must be capitalized and recovered over time through depreciation rather than deducted all at once.
summary: Taxable rental income includes rent, advance rent, lease-cancellation payments, tenant-paid expenses, and kept security deposits.
Deductible operating expenses: mortgage interest, property taxes, insurance, utilities, management fees, HOA dues, advertising, repairs.
Repairs (keep property in operating condition) are currently deductible; improvements are capital costs.
Capital improvements must be capitalized and depreciated over time, not expensed in one year.

modules.title: Unit 2: Depreciation of Rental Property
modules.body:
Depreciation lets an owner recover the cost of income-producing property over its useful life. Under the Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS), residential rental buildings placed in service after 1986 are depreciated using the straight-line method over a recovery period of 27.5 years. (Commercial real property uses a longer 39-year period.) Straight-line means roughly equal deductions each full year.

Land is never depreciable because it does not wear out or become obsolete. Therefore an owner must allocate the purchase price (the basis) between the non-depreciable land and the depreciable building. A common approach is to use the assessed-value ratio from the property tax bill, but other reasonable methods (such as an appraisal) may be used; only the building portion is depreciated.

Depreciation begins when the property is "placed in service" — when it is ready and available to be rented, not necessarily when a tenant actually moves in or when you bought it. MACRS uses a mid-month convention for real property, meaning the first and last months are pro-rated as if the property was placed in service at the midpoint of the month.
summary: Residential rental property is depreciated straight-line over 27.5 years under MACRS (verify current rules).
Land is NOT depreciable; only the building/improvement portion of basis can be depreciated.
Allocate basis between land and building, often using the property-tax assessed-value ratio.
Depreciation starts when the property is 'placed in service' (ready and available to rent).

modules.title: Unit 3: Passive Activity Loss Rules
modules.body:
Rental real estate is generally treated as a passive activity, even if the owner is involved in managing it. Losses from passive activities can usually only offset income from other passive activities; they cannot freely offset wages, salary, or portfolio income such as interest and dividends. This limits the immediate tax benefit of a rental loss for many owners.

There is an important exception: the special $25,000 allowance. A taxpayer who 'actively participates' in the rental (a less demanding standard than material participation — for example, approving tenants, setting rents, and making management decisions) may deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against non-passive income. This allowance phases out as modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) rises between $100,000 and $150,000, reducing by $0.50 for every $1.00 of MAGI over $100,000, and is fully gone at $150,000.

Losses that are disallowed in a given year are not lost forever. Suspended passive losses carry forward indefinitely to future years, where they can offset future passive income, be freed by the $25,000 allowance, or be fully deducted when the owner disposes of the entire interest in the activity in a taxable sale.
summary: Rental real estate is generally a passive activity; passive losses usually offset only passive income.
Special $25,000 allowance lets active participants deduct rental losses against other income (verify current figure).
The allowance phases out for MAGI between $100,000 and $150,000, gone entirely at $150,000.
Suspended (disallowed) passive losses carry forward and can be used later or upon full disposition.

modules.title: Unit 4: Sale of Rental Property — Capital Gains & Deferral
modules.body:
When a rental is sold, gain or loss is the amount realized (sale price minus selling costs) minus the property's adjusted basis. Adjusted basis starts with original cost, increases for capital improvements, and decreases for depreciation taken (or that should have been taken). Because depreciation lowers basis, it increases the eventual gain.

Gain on property held more than one year is generally long-term capital gain, taxed at favorable rates. However, the portion of gain attributable to depreciation is subject to depreciation recapture. For residential rental real estate, this is 'unrecaptured Section 1250 gain,' which is taxed at a maximum rate of 25% — higher than the regular long-term capital gains rate but still below ordinary rates. Recapture exists so taxpayers cannot get an ordinary deduction for depreciation and then pay only low capital-gains rates on that same amount.

An owner can defer paying tax on the gain through a Section 1031 like-kind exchange, in which qualifying real property held for business or investment is exchanged for other like-kind real property, subject to strict timing and identification rules (generally 45 days to identify and 180 days to close). Note that after the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, only real property qualifies for 1031 treatment — personal property no longer does. These transactions are complex; always consult a qualified tax professional.
summary: Gain = amount realized minus adjusted basis; depreciation reduces basis and thus increases gain.
Long-term gains get favorable rates, but depreciation recapture (unrecaptured Sec. 1250 gain) is taxed up to 25%.
A Section 1031 like-kind exchange can defer gain; strict 45-day ID and 180-day closing rules apply.
After 2017, only real property qualifies for 1031 exchanges; consult a tax professional before acting.

m1.q: Which of the following is generally NOT taxable rental income when received?
choices: Advance rent paid by a tenant
A refundable security deposit you intend to return
A payment from a tenant to cancel a lease
The value of services a tenant provides in place of rent
m1.explanation: A refundable security deposit is not income when received; it becomes income only if you keep it.

m1.q: Which expense must be capitalized and depreciated rather than deducted in the current year?
m1.explanation: A new roof is a capital improvement that betters the property, so it must be capitalized and depreciated.

m1.q: Which of these is a currently deductible operating expense for a rental?
choices: An addition that adds a new bedroom
m1.explanation: Property-management fees are an ordinary operating expense deductible in the year incurred; the others are capital or non-depreciable costs.

m1.q: A landlord receives a $1,200 security deposit that will be refunded to the tenant at the end of the lease if no damage occurs. How is this deposit treated in the year received?
choices: It is not taxable rental income because it is held as a refundable deposit
It is fully taxable as rental income in the year received
It is taxable only to the extent it exceeds one month's rent
It is treated as a nontaxable loan and never reported
m1.explanation: A refundable security deposit the landlord intends to return is not income when received; it becomes income only if kept (e.g., applied to unpaid rent or damages).

m1.q: Which of the following expenditures is a deductible repair rather than a capital improvement?
choices: Replacing the entire roof of the rental house
Adding a new central air-conditioning system
Fixing a leaking faucet and patching a small section of drywall
m1.explanation: Routine fixes that keep the property in ordinary operating condition, like repairing a faucet, are currently deductible repairs, while items that add value or prolong useful life must be capitalized.

m1.q: A tenant pays the landlord's water bill directly and deducts that amount from the rent. How should the landlord treat the payment of that expense?
choices: The amount is ignored because the tenant paid it, not the landlord
The landlord includes the amount as rental income and may deduct it as a utility expense
The landlord deducts it but does not report any income
The landlord reports it as a tax-free reimbursement
m1.explanation: When a tenant pays an expense in lieu of rent, the landlord must include that amount in rental income and may then deduct the same amount as the related operating expense.

m2.q: Over what recovery period is residential rental property depreciated under MACRS?
m2.explanation: Residential rental property uses a 27.5-year straight-line recovery period under MACRS (39 years is for commercial).

m2.q: Which portion of a rental purchase generally CANNOT be depreciated?
m2.explanation: Land does not wear out or become obsolete, so it is never depreciable.

m2.q: Depreciation on a rental begins when the property is:
choices: Purchased and the deed is recorded
Placed in service (ready and available to rent)
m2.explanation: Depreciation starts when the property is placed in service — ready and available to rent — not necessarily at purchase or occupancy.

m2.q: A residential rental building (excluding land) has a depreciable basis of $275,000. What is the approximate annual straight-line MACRS depreciation deduction once fully placed in service?
m2.explanation: Residential rental property is depreciated straight-line over 27.5 years, so $275,000 / 27.5 = $10,000 per year.

m2.q: Under what convention is residential rental real property generally treated as placed in service for MACRS depreciation purposes?
m2.explanation: Real property under MACRS uses the mid-month convention, treating it as placed in service at the midpoint of the month regardless of the actual date.

m2.q: An investor buys a rental property for $400,000, with the land valued at $100,000 and the building at $300,000. What amount is subject to depreciation?
m2.explanation: Land is not depreciable, so only the building's allocated basis of $300,000 may be depreciated.

m3.q: Rental real estate is generally classified for tax purposes as a:
m3.explanation: Rental real estate is generally a passive activity, so its losses are subject to the passive activity loss limits.

m3.q: The special $25,000 allowance for rental losses begins to phase out when MAGI exceeds:
m3.explanation: The $25,000 allowance phases out for MAGI between $100,000 and $150,000, disappearing entirely at $150,000.

m3.q: What happens to passive rental losses that are disallowed in the current year?
choices: They carry forward to future years
They must be refunded to the tenant
m3.explanation: Suspended passive losses carry forward indefinitely and can be used against future passive income or upon full disposition.

m3.q: A taxpayer who actively participates in a rental activity may deduct up to what amount of rental losses against nonpassive income, subject to MAGI phaseout?
m3.explanation: The special allowance lets actively participating taxpayers deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against other income, subject to the MAGI phaseout.

m3.q: At what modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) is the $25,000 special rental loss allowance completely phased out?
m3.explanation: The allowance phases out at 50 cents per dollar of MAGI over $100,000 and is fully eliminated once MAGI reaches $150,000.

m3.q: A rental loss that cannot be deducted in the current year because of the passive activity loss rules is treated how?
choices: It is permanently lost and never deductible
It is suspended and carried forward to offset future passive income or gain on disposition
It must be carried back three years
It converts into a deductible capital loss
m3.explanation: Disallowed passive losses are suspended and carried forward indefinitely, usable against future passive income or when the activity is fully disposed of.

m4.q: How does depreciation taken over the years affect the property's adjusted basis at sale?
choices: It has no effect on basis
It decreases basis, increasing taxable gain
m4.explanation: Depreciation reduces adjusted basis, which increases the taxable gain when the property is sold.

m4.q: Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain on residential rental property is taxed at a maximum rate of:
m4.explanation: Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain (the depreciation portion) is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%.

m4.q: After the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, a Section 1031 like-kind exchange may be used only for:
choices: Real property held for business or investment
m4.explanation: Post-2017, only real property held for business or investment qualifies for Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange treatment.

m4.q: At what maximum federal rate is unrecaptured Section 1250 gain (attributable to prior depreciation on real property) taxed?
m4.explanation: Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain from depreciation taken on real property is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%.

m4.q: Under current law (post-2017), which type of property still qualifies for a tax-deferred Section 1031 like-kind exchange?
choices: Real property held for business or investment
m4.explanation: After the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, only real property held for productive use in a trade or business or for investment qualifies for Section 1031 deferral.

m4.q: An investor sells a rental property. The adjusted basis used to compute gain is best described as the original cost:
choices: Plus capital improvements, minus accumulated depreciation
m4.explanation: Adjusted basis equals original cost increased by capital improvements and decreased by depreciation taken, which determines the gain on sale.

m1.front: What counts as taxable rental income?
m1.back: Rent, advance rent, lease-cancellation payments, tenant-paid expenses, and the value of property/services received in place of rent.
Not when received if you plan to return them; a deposit you keep (for damages or unpaid rent) becomes income at that point.
Mortgage interest, property taxes, insurance, utilities, management fees, HOA dues, advertising, repairs, supplies.
A repair keeps property in operating condition and is currently deductible; an improvement betters/restores it and must be capitalized.

m1.front: Where are rental income and expenses reported?
m1.back: Generally on Schedule E (Supplemental Income and Loss).

m2.front: Recovery period for residential rental property
m2.back: 27.5 years, straight-line, under MACRS (verify current rules).
No — land never wears out or becomes obsolete, so only the building portion is depreciated.
Split purchase price between non-depreciable land and the depreciable building, often using the property-tax assessed-value ratio.
When the property is ready and available to be rented — when depreciation begins.
Straight-line MACRS with a mid-month convention.

m3.front: How is rental real estate generally classified?
m3.back: As a passive activity, even if the owner manages it.
Passive losses generally offset only passive income, not wages or portfolio income.
Active participants may deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against other income (verify current figure).
Phases out for MAGI between $100,000 and $150,000; fully gone at $150,000.
Carry forward indefinitely; usable against future passive income or when the activity is fully disposed of.

m4.back: Original cost plus capital improvements, minus depreciation taken (or that should have been taken).
The depreciation portion of gain ('unrecaptured Section 1250 gain') is taxed at a maximum of 25%.
Gain on property held over one year is generally long-term capital gain taxed at favorable rates.
Defers gain by exchanging investment real property for like-kind real property; 45-day ID and 180-day closing rules apply.
Only real property qualifies; personal property no longer does. Consult a tax professional.
